description:
You are an observability engineer who has just been given a raw set of latency logs and a stub-configuration file for a Grafana dashboard.  
    Your goal is to do two things, entirely with standard Unix text–processing utilities (awk, sed, sort, etc.) and without writing any ad-hoc scripts:

    1. Produce a **daily service-level CSV summary** from the raw log files that live in
       /home/user/metrics/raw/ .  
       Every log line is already guaranteed to have this exact 4-column, single-space-separated format:

           ISO8601-timestamp  serviceName  latencyMs  httpStatusCode

       • There will never be comment lines, blank lines or headers.  
       • All log files in the directory belong to the same date.  
       • The date can be inferred from the filename pattern <service>-YYYY-MM-DD.log.  
       • For every distinct serviceName you must calculate the following aggregates:
         – total_requests  (all lines)  
         – success_requests (httpStatusCode strictly < 400)  
         – error_requests   (httpStatusCode ≥ 400)  
         – avg_latency_ms   (arithmetic mean of the latencyMs column, **rounded to the nearest integer**; you may rely on awk’s built-in integer rounding by adding 0.5 and using int())  

       • Write the final report to the path
         /home/user/metrics/processed/YYYY-MM-DD_metrics_summary.csv  
         where YYYY-MM-DD is the date you inferred from the filenames.  
       • The CSV MUST match **exactly** this 5-column, comma-separated, no-spaces format (including header line):

           service,total_requests,success_requests,error_requests,avg_latency_ms
           serviceA,123,120,3,87
           serviceB,45,45,0,102
           … (one line per service, sorted alphabetically by service name)

       • The automated grader checks:
         – that the file exists at the exact path,  
         – that the header line is present and correct,  
         – that every service found in the raw logs appears exactly once,  
         – that the numeric aggregates are correct.

    2. Generate a **cleaned Grafana dashboard configuration** out of the template file located at
       /home/user/grafana/dashboards/sample_dashboard.conf  

       The template always contains exactly three lines (no blank lines) in this order:

           cpu_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_CPU_THRESHOLD
           mem_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_MEM_THRESHOLD
           disk_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_DISK_THRESHOLD

       Replace the placeholder tokens with the following integer limits:

           cpu_threshold   → 80
           mem_threshold   → 75
           disk_threshold  → 90

       Strip any leading or trailing whitespace that might be present on any of the three lines,
       keep the original line order, and save the result to

            /home/user/grafana/dashboards/sample_dashboard.conf.updated

       The automated grader checks that:
         • The *.updated* file exists at the exact path.  
         • It contains exactly three newline-terminated lines, **no extra blank lines**.  
         • The lines read, in order:

             cpu_threshold=80
             mem_threshold=75
             disk_threshold=90

    Operational constraints:
    • You do NOT have sudo or root. Everything must run as the regular user.  
    • Use only standard POSIX command-line tools that come with a minimal Linux install.  
    • Do NOT hard-code the date; extract it from the filenames.  
    • Do NOT manually type the output; generate it programmatically with awk/sed/…  
    • When you are done, you should have created exactly two new files:
        /home/user/metrics/processed/YYYY-MM-DD_metrics_summary.csv
        /home/user/grafana/dashboards/sample_dashboard.conf.updated

    Deliverables: none besides those two files. You may display console output while working, but the grader only inspects the filesystem.
truth:
<!--
      Pre-existing filesystem before the agent starts:
      ------------------------------------------------
      /home/user/
      ├── grafana/
      │   └── dashboards/
      │       └── sample_dashboard.conf
      └── metrics/
          └── raw/
              ├── serviceA-2023-10-01.log
              └── serviceB-2023-10-01.log

      Contents of the raw log files (single space separated):
      --------------------------------------------------------
      File: /home/user/metrics/raw/serviceA-2023-10-01.log

      2023-10-01T00:00:01Z serviceA 120 200
      2023-10-01T00:00:02Z serviceA 250 200
      2023-10-01T00:00:03Z serviceA 110 500

      File: /home/user/metrics/raw/serviceB-2023-10-01.log

      2023-10-01T00:00:01Z serviceB 95 200
      2023-10-01T00:00:02Z serviceB 105 200

      Contents of the template dashboard file:
      ----------------------------------------
      File: /home/user/grafana/dashboards/sample_dashboard.conf

      cpu_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_CPU_THRESHOLD
      mem_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_MEM_THRESHOLD
      disk_threshold=PLACEHOLDER_DISK_THRESHOLD


      Expected artefacts after the agent finishes:
      --------------------------------------------
      1. /home/user/metrics/processed/2023-10-01_metrics_summary.csv

         Header line first, followed by one line per service in alphabetical order:

           service,total_requests,success_requests,error_requests,avg_latency_ms
           serviceA,3,2,1,160
           serviceB,2,2,0,100

         (Avg latency: serviceA (120+250+110)/3 = 160; serviceB (95+105)/2 = 100)

      2. /home/user/grafana/dashboards/sample_dashboard.conf.updated

           cpu_threshold=80
           mem_threshold=75
           disk_threshold=90

      The automated test will verify the existence and exact content of the above two files.
    -->
